title: remove $-prefix from (most) shell examples
body:
For the shell examples related to building and installing podman, remove the shell prompt indicator character '$'.

This makes copying and pasting lines much easier.

Retain the prompt indicator for the post-install shell examples, since they (often) mix shell commands and shell command output, so it serves a useful purpose to distinguish them.

comments.body:
Can one of the admins verify this patch?
I understand the following commands:
- `bot, add author to whitelist`
- `bot, test pull request`
- `bot, test pull request once`
@TomSweeneyRedHat I think those were in to show commands were intended to be run as non-root, right?

The sudo would indicate it, I am fine with this change.
Thanks @jmtd 
/approve
bot, add author to whitelist

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
@mheon, yes they were put there to show non-root.  I understand removing them for cut/paste flexibility, but I'd prefer to keep them in the document for clarity.
@TomSweeneyRedHat You don't think the sudo makes it clear you are doing this as non root?  Could we just add a line above this section that the following commands are done as non root?

@rhatdan This is one of those 6 of one, half dozen of another things. if we keep it without this change, some folks won't like the cut/paste hassle.  If we change it, other folks won't see the sudo and will report errors that the example doesn't work.

 I'm fine with the change as is with the sudo, an added line of explanation would be good.   If it becomes a hassle problem reporting wise, we can revert.  It's a matter of picking your poison.
@jmtd Could you add a NOTE at the the front of the code indicating that this code is expected to be run as NON root.

@rhatdan sure, done, thanks for the suggestion. I've also gone through and more thoroughly eliminated the $-prefixes, in all but the cases where the code blocks list both commands and command output, where I left them in to distinguish the two. In particular I nixed two (inexplicable) hash prefixes that were used for the `curl` examples.

[ I've successfully got `podman` running on a Debian host, now, and I've been pondering the likelyhood of a packaging effort  completing in time for Debian's next stable code freeze, which is January, I think ]
LGTM, thanks for the fix ups.  Tests aren't happy, but I can't imagine it's due to this change.
